era_context: The late medieval period continues, with European nation-states consolidating power. The Ottoman Empire is at its zenith under Mehmed II. In East Asia, the Ming Dynasty maintains centralised rule. The Age of Exploration is accelerating, with Portuguese expeditions advancing down the African coast. In the Americas, the Aztec and Inca empires are at their height.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (minimal written records; Portuguese sources are limited and biased)
Southeast Asia (sparse European documentation; local chronicles underrepresented)
Pacific Islands and Oceania (no written records)

events.title: Battle of Towton: Decisive Yorkist victory in the Wars of the Roses
events.description: On 29 March 1460 (some sources date this to 1461), the Battle of Towton was fought between Yorkist and Lancastrian forces near the village of Towton in Yorkshire. The Yorkists, led by Edward IV and the Earl of Warwick, achieved a decisive victory, killing an estimated 20,000–28,000 soldiers (making it one of the bloodiest battles in English history). The victory secured Edward IV's claim to the English throne and marked a turning point in the Wars of the Roses, though Lancastrian resistance would persist for years. The battle's scale and brutality exemplified the intensity of the civil conflict.
key_figures: Edward IV (Yorkist claimant, future King of England)
Richard Neville, 16th Earl of Warwick (Yorkist commander)
Henry VI (Lancastrian King, deposed after the battle)

sources.name: Chronicles of the Wars of the Roses (various contemporary and near-contemporary accounts)

events.certainty_note: The Battle of Towton is one of the most thoroughly documented events of the Wars of the Roses. The date of 29 March 1460 is well-established, though some sources place it in 1461. Modern scholarship confirms the date as 29 March 1461 (not 1460), making this entry technically misdated.

events.title: Siege of Roxburgh Castle: Death of James II of Scotland
events.description: During the Siege of Roxburgh Castle in August 1460, King James II of Scotland was killed by a cannon explosion. The castle was being besieged by Scottish forces attempting to recapture it from English control. James II's death at age 29 was a significant blow to Scottish independence efforts. His young son, James III, succeeded him, leading to a regency period marked by factional disputes among Scottish nobles. The siege itself was eventually successful, with the castle being recaptured by Scottish forces.
key_figures: Mary of Guelders (Queen Mother, regent for James III)
James Kennedy (Bishop of St Andrews, regent)

sources.name: Scottish royal charters and administrative records

events.certainty_note: James II's death during the Siege of Roxburgh Castle in August 1460 is well-documented in Scottish and English sources. The date is secure.

events.title: Portuguese exploration: Expansion of trading posts on the African coast
events.description: Portuguese explorers and traders, under the patronage of Prince Henry the Navigator, continued to establish trading posts and forts along the West African coast. In 1460, the Portuguese were consolidating their presence in the region, particularly in present-day Guinea and Sierra Leone. These posts served as bases for trade in gold, ivory, and enslaved people, and as launching points for further exploration southward. The Portuguese were systematically mapping the African coast and establishing commercial networks that would eventually lead to the rounding of the Cape of Good Hope.
key_figures: Prince Henry the Navigator (Portuguese royal patron)
Portuguese merchants and explorers (names often unrecorded)

sources.name: Crónica da Guiné by Gomes Eanes de Zurara (contemporary Portuguese chronicle)
Portuguese royal charters and trading records

events.certainty_note: Portuguese expansion in West Africa during the 1460s is well-documented in Portuguese sources. Specific events in 1460 are less precisely dated; the broader pattern of expansion is confirmed.

events.title: Ming Dynasty: Zhengtong Emperor's restoration (Tianshun Emperor's reign)
events.description: In 1460, the Ming Dynasty was ruled by the Tianshun Emperor (r. 1457–1464), who had been restored to the throne after a period of captivity and exile. The Tianshun Emperor's reign was marked by political instability and the growing influence of eunuchs at court. The period saw continued consolidation of Ming power, though the court was increasingly divided by factional disputes. The Tianshun Emperor's health was declining, and succession disputes were beginning to emerge.
key_figures: Eunuch factions at the Ming court
events.certainty_note: The Ming Dynasty's political structure and succession are well-documented in official records. The Tianshun Emperor's reign (1457–1464) is secure.

events.title: Ottoman Empire: Mehmed II's campaigns in Anatolia and the Balkans
events.description: Mehmed II continued his campaigns to consolidate Ottoman power and eliminate rival Turkish beyliks in Anatolia. In 1460, the Ottoman Empire was expanding its territorial control and strengthening its position as the dominant regional power. Mehmed II was also preparing for the conquest of Trebizond, which would occur in 1461. The period was marked by military campaigns, diplomatic negotiations, and the consolidation of Ottoman administrative structures.

sources.name: Ottoman administrative records and diplomatic correspondence

events.certainty_note: Ottoman expansion during the 1460s is well-documented in Ottoman sources. Specific events in 1460 are less precisely dated; the broader pattern of consolidation is confirmed.

events.title: Inca Empire: Pachacuti's reign continues; expansion and consolidation
events.description: Pachacuti Inca Yupanqui continued his reign as the Inca Emperor, overseeing the expansion and consolidation of the Inca Empire. In 1460, the empire was at the height of its territorial reach, with Pachacuti conducting military campaigns to extend Inca control over new regions and consolidate tributary relationships. The Inca administrative system was being refined, with the quipu (knotted cord recording system) being used to track tribute and census information. Pachacuti's reign was marked by significant architectural and administrative achievements.

sources.name: Quipucamayoc (Inca oral tradition, recorded by Spanish chroniclers)
Spanish colonial chronicles (Garcilaso de la Vega, Cobo)

events.certainty_note: Inca chronology is uncertain. Pachacuti's reign is broadly dated to 1438–1471, but specific events within years are speculative. No contemporary written records exist.

events.title: Aztec Empire: Montezuma I's reign; military campaigns and tributary expansion
events.description: Montezuma I continued his reign as the Aztec Emperor, conducting military campaigns to expand the empire's territorial reach and extract tribute from subject peoples. In 1460, the Aztec Empire was consolidating its hegemony over Mesoamerica, with Tenochtitlan growing in size and power. Montezuma I's reign was marked by military success, administrative innovation, and the development of the tributary system that would define Aztec imperial power.

sources.name: Codex Mendoza (Aztec pictorial record, compiled post-conquest)

events.certainty_note: Aztec chronology is based on indigenous sources recorded by Spanish chroniclers after the conquest. The broad outline of Montezuma I's reign is accepted, but specific events within years are often uncertain.
cross_references: 1469-001 (Montezuma I's death and succession of Axayacatl)

disconfirming_evidence: The Battle of Towton is traditionally dated to 29 March 1460, but modern scholarship increasingly places it on 29 March 1461. This discrepancy reflects the complexity of medieval dating conventions and the reliance on near-contemporary chronicles that sometimes conflated years. The significance of the battle as a 'turning point' in the Wars of the Roses is somewhat overstated; Lancastrian resistance persisted for over a decade, and Edward IV's position remained contested. The Portuguese expansion in West Africa (1460-003) is often portrayed as a purely commercial and exploratory enterprise, but it was fundamentally tied to the slave trade and the exploitation of African resources, a dimension that is sometimes minimised in traditional historiography.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1460 is heavily weighted towards Europe, where written documentation is abundant. The Battle of Towton is one of the most thoroughly documented events of the year, with multiple contemporary and near-contemporary accounts. East Asian sources (Ming Dynasty records) are rich but often focused on court politics. The Americas are represented only through post-conquest indigenous sources and Spanish chronicles, which are inherently biased. Sub-Saharan Africa is almost entirely absent from the historical record at this date, with only Portuguese sources providing limited information about coastal regions. The reliability of dating for non-European events is significantly lower than for European events.

graph_edges.note: The Battle of Towton led directly to Edward IV's consolidation of power in 1461.
James II's death in 1460 led to James III's succession and regency in 1461.
Mehmed II's campaigns in 1460 culminated in the conquest of Trebizond in 1461.
